chunks.text: A Novel Self-Supervised Deep Learning Method for MRI Reconstruction (EECS-2024-170) by Frederic Wang. This technical report presents a self-supervised deep learning approach for MRI reconstruction, focusing on improving image quality without requiring fully sampled training data. The method leverages unlabeled data to learn reconstruction patterns, potentially reducing scan times and enhancing diagnostic accuracy in clinical settings.

Respiratory and Cardiac Motion Correction Using the Beat Pilot Tone (EECS-2023-169) by Katie Lamar. This report describes a technique using the Beat Pilot Tone to correct for respiratory and cardiac motion in MRI scans. It aims to improve image clarity by compensating for physiological movements, which is crucial for accurate diagnosis in dynamic imaging applications.

Deep Learning Applications in Computational MRI: A Thesis in Two Parts (EECS-2021-91) by Sukrit Arora. This thesis explores deep learning applications in computational MRI, divided into two parts. It covers advanced algorithms and models that enhance image reconstruction and analysis, contributing to faster and more efficient MRI processing techniques.

Integrating a Localized B0 Shim Array into a Solenoid Transmit-Receive Coil for Permanent Magnet Scanners (EECS-2021-87) by Celine Veys and Rafael Calleja. This technical report details the integration of a localized B0 shim array into a solenoid transmit-receive coil for permanent magnet MRI scanners. It focuses on improving magnetic field homogeneity to enhance image quality in low-field systems.

Local B0 Shim Array Integrated onto a Solenoid TRX Coil for Permanent Magnet Scanners (EECS-2021-84) by Rafael Calleja and Celine Veys. This report describes the integration of a local B0 shim array onto a solenoid transmit-receive coil for permanent magnet MRI scanners. It aims to optimize magnetic field uniformity, potentially leading to better imaging performance in compact scanner designs.

Node-Pore Coded Coincidence Correcting Microfluidic Channel Framework: Code Design and Sparse Deconvolution (EECS-2017-213) by Michael Kellman. This technical report presents a node-pore coded framework for microfluidic channels, focusing on code design and sparse deconvolution techniques. It applies to MRI-related microfluidic applications, enhancing signal processing and analysis in fluid-based imaging systems.

Parallelism, Patterns, and Performance in Iterative MRI Reconstruction (EECS-2011-134) by Mark Murphy. This report examines parallelism, patterns, and performance in iterative MRI reconstruction algorithms. It discusses computational strategies to accelerate reconstruction processes, improving efficiency and scalability for high-resolution imaging in clinical and research settings.
